notes.heading: Introduction
notes.text: This chapter deals with organic compounds containing oxygen. Alcohols, phenols and ethers are important classes of organic compounds.
points: Alcohols contain the hydroxyl group –OH attached to a saturated alkyl carbon. Example: ethanol, CH3CH2OH.
Phenols contain the –OH group directly attached to an aromatic ring, usually benzene. Example: phenol, C6H5OH.
Ethers contain an oxygen atom bonded to two alkyl or aryl groups. General formula: R–O–R′. Example: dimethyl ether, CH3–O–CH3.
Alcohols and phenols both contain –OH, but in alcohols –OH is attached to an alkyl carbon, while in phenols –OH is directly attached to a benzene ring.
Ethers contain –O– between two carbon groups and do not contain an O–H bond.

notes.heading: Classification of Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
notes.text: Alcohols, phenols and ethers are classified on the basis of the group attached to oxygen or the number of hydroxyl groups present.
points: Alcohols are classified according to the number of carbon atoms attached to the carbon bearing the –OH group.
Primary alcohols have the –OH carbon attached to one carbon atom. General formula: R–CH2–OH. Example: ethanol, CH3CH2OH.
Secondary alcohols have the –OH carbon attached to two carbon atoms. General formula: R–CH(OH)–R′. Example: propan-2-ol, CH3–CH(OH)–CH3.
Tertiary alcohols have the –OH carbon attached to three carbon atoms. General formula: R3C–OH. Example: 2-methylpropan-2-ol, (CH3)3C–OH.
Phenols are classified according to the number of –OH groups attached to the benzene ring.
Monohydric phenols contain one –OH group. Example: phenol, C6H5OH.
Dihydric phenols contain two –OH groups. Examples: catechol or 1,2-dihydroxybenzene, resorcinol or 1,3-dihydroxybenzene, and hydroquinone or 1,4-dihydroxybenzene.
Trihydric phenols contain three –OH groups. Example: pyrogallol or 1,2,3-trihydroxybenzene.
Ethers are classified based on the groups attached to oxygen. General formula: R–O–R′.
Simple or symmetrical ethers have the same groups on both sides of oxygen. Example: dimethyl ether, CH3–O–CH3.
Mixed or unsymmetrical ethers have different groups on both sides of oxygen. Example: ethyl methyl ether, CH3–O–C2H5.

notes.heading: Nomenclature of Alcohols
notes.text: Alcohols are named by selecting the longest chain containing the –OH group and replacing the final -e of the alkane with -ol.
points: The general formula of alcohols is R–OH.
In IUPAC nomenclature, alkane names are changed to alkanol names. Example: ethane becomes ethanol.
The longest carbon chain containing the –OH group is selected as the parent chain.
The chain is numbered from the end nearer to the –OH group.
The position of the –OH group is mentioned before the suffix -ol.
Examples: CH3OH is methanol; CH3CH2OH is ethanol; CH3CH2CH2OH is propan-1-ol; CH3CH(OH)CH3 is propan-2-ol.
In common naming, alcohols are named as alkyl alcohols. Examples: CH3OH is methyl alcohol, C2H5OH is ethyl alcohol, CH3CH2CH2OH is n-propyl alcohol, and CH3CH(OH)CH3 is isopropyl alcohol.

notes.heading: Nomenclature of Phenols
notes.text: Phenols are named as hydroxy derivatives of benzene or as substituted phenols.
points: The simplest phenol is C6H5OH, called phenol or hydroxybenzene.
When another group is attached to the benzene ring along with –OH, positions are shown as ortho, meta and para.
Ortho or o- means 1,2-position.
Meta or m- means 1,3-position.
Para or p- means 1,4-position.
o-Cresol has –OH and –CH3 at 1,2-positions and its IUPAC name is 2-methylphenol.
m-Cresol has –OH and –CH3 at 1,3-positions and its IUPAC name is 3-methylphenol.
p-Cresol has –OH and –CH3 at 1,4-positions and its IUPAC name is 4-methylphenol.
Catechol is benzene-1,2-diol.
Resorcinol is benzene-1,3-diol.
Hydroquinone is benzene-1,4-diol.

notes.heading: Nomenclature of Ethers
notes.text: Ethers are named in IUPAC system as alkoxyalkanes.
points: The general structure of ethers is R–O–R′.
The smaller alkyl group attached to oxygen is treated as an alkoxy group.
Common alkoxy groups are CH3O– as methoxy, C2H5O– as ethoxy, and C3H7O– as propoxy.
CH3–O–CH3 is methoxymethane in IUPAC nomenclature and dimethyl ether in common nomenclature.
CH3–O–CH2CH3 is methoxyethane in IUPAC nomenclature and ethyl methyl ether in common nomenclature.
C2H5–O–C2H5 is ethoxyethane in IUPAC nomenclature and diethyl ether in common nomenclature.

notes.heading: Preparation of Alcohols from Alkenes
notes.text: Alcohols can be prepared from alkenes by addition of water across the double bond.
points: The addition of water to an alkene is called hydration of alkenes.
In acid-catalysed hydration, alkene reacts with water in the presence of acid to form alcohol.
General reaction: Alkene + H2O → Alcohol.
Example: CH2=CH2 + H2O → CH3CH2OH. Ethene gives ethanol.
Acid-catalysed hydration follows Markovnikov’s rule.
Markovnikov’s rule states that in the addition of water to an unsymmetrical alkene, H+ goes to the carbon already having more hydrogen atoms, and –OH goes to the carbon having fewer hydrogen atoms.
Example: CH3–CH=CH2 + H2O → CH3–CH(OH)–CH3. Propene gives propan-2-ol.
Hydroboration-oxidation of alkenes gives alcohols by anti-Markovnikov addition.
Reagents for hydroboration-oxidation are B2H6 followed by H2O2/OH−.
In hydroboration-oxidation, –OH goes to the carbon having more hydrogen atoms.
Example: CH3–CH=CH2 → CH3–CH2–CH2OH. Propene gives propan-1-ol.
From propene, acid hydration gives propan-2-ol, while hydroboration-oxidation gives propan-1-ol.

notes.heading: Preparation of Alcohols from Carbonyl Compounds, Carboxylic Acids, Esters and Grignard Reagents
notes.text: Alcohols are prepared by reduction of carbonyl compounds, carboxylic acids and esters, and by reaction of carbonyl compounds with Grignard reagents followed by hydrolysis.
points: Aldehydes and ketones are carbonyl compounds.
Reduction of aldehydes gives primary alcohols. General reaction: R–CHO → R–CH2OH.
Example: CH3CHO → CH3CH2OH. Ethanal gives ethanol.
Reduction of ketones gives secondary alcohols. General reaction: R–CO–R′ → R–CH(OH)–R′.
Example: CH3COCH3 → CH3CH(OH)CH3. Propanone gives propan-2-ol.
Common reducing agents are LiAlH4, NaBH4 and H2/Ni.
Carboxylic acids are reduced to primary alcohols using LiAlH4. General reaction: R–COOH → R–CH2OH.
Example: CH3COOH → CH3CH2OH. Ethanoic acid gives ethanol.
Esters give two alcohol molecules on reduction. General reaction: R–COOR′ → R–CH2OH + R′OH.
Example: CH3COOC2H5 → CH3CH2OH + C2H5OH. Ethyl ethanoate gives ethanol.
Grignard reagents have the general formula R–MgX. Example: CH3MgBr.
Formaldehyde reacts with a Grignard reagent followed by hydrolysis to give a primary alcohol. Reaction: HCHO + RMgX → RCH2OH after hydrolysis.
Example: HCHO + CH3MgBr → CH3CH2OH after hydrolysis.
Aldehydes other than formaldehyde react with Grignard reagents followed by hydrolysis to give secondary alcohols.
Example: CH3CHO + CH3MgBr → CH3CH(OH)CH3 after hydrolysis.
Ketones react with Grignard reagents followed by hydrolysis to give tertiary alcohols.
Example: CH3COCH3 + CH3MgBr → (CH3)3COH after hydrolysis.

notes.heading: Preparation of Phenols
notes.text: Phenols can be prepared from haloarenes, benzene sulphonic acid, diazonium salts and cumene.
points: Phenols have the general formula Ar–OH. Example: phenol, C6H5OH.
Phenol can be prepared from chlorobenzene by heating it with NaOH at high temperature and pressure to form sodium phenoxide, followed by acidification.
Reaction: C6H5Cl + NaOH → C6H5ONa.
Reaction: C6H5ONa + HCl → C6H5OH + NaCl.
Conditions for conversion of chlorobenzene to sodium phenoxide are about 623 K and 300 atm.
Chlorobenzene needs harsh conditions because the C–Cl bond has partial double bond character due to resonance.
Phenol can be prepared from benzene sulphonic acid by heating sodium benzene sulphonate with molten NaOH to form sodium phenoxide, followed by acidification.
Reaction: C6H5SO3Na + NaOH → C6H5ONa.
Reaction: C6H5ONa + HCl → C6H5OH.
Phenol can be prepared from benzene diazonium chloride by reaction with warm water.
Reaction: C6H5N2+Cl− + H2O → C6H5OH + N2 + HCl.
Nitrogen gas is released in the hydrolysis of diazonium salt.
Phenol can be prepared industrially from cumene or isopropyl benzene.
Cumene is oxidised to cumene hydroperoxide, which on acid treatment gives phenol and acetone.
Reaction: Cumene + O2 → Cumene hydroperoxide.
Reaction: Cumene hydroperoxide → Phenol + Acetone.
The cumene process is important industrially because it gives two useful products: phenol and acetone.

notes.heading: Preparation of Ethers
notes.text: Ethers are prepared by dehydration of alcohols and by Williamson ether synthesis.
points: Ethers have the general formula R–O–R′.
Alcohols form ethers when heated with concentrated H2SO4 at about 413 K.
This reaction is called intermolecular dehydration.
General reaction: 2R–OH → R–O–R + H2O.
Example: 2CH3CH2OH → CH3CH2–O–CH2CH3 + H2O. Ethanol gives ethoxyethane or diethyl ether.
At lower temperature, alcohol gives ether, while at higher temperature, alcohol gives alkene.
Alcohol + conc. H2SO4 at 413 K gives ether.
Alcohol + conc. H2SO4 at 443 K gives alkene.
Williamson ether synthesis is an important method for preparing ethers.
In Williamson synthesis, sodium alkoxide reacts with alkyl halide.
General reaction: R–O−Na+ + R′–X → R–O–R′ + NaX.
Example: CH3ONa + CH3Br → CH3–O–CH3 + NaBr. Sodium methoxide gives dimethyl ether.
Example: C2H5ONa + CH3Br → CH3–O–C2H5 + NaBr. Product is methoxyethane.
Aryl alkyl ethers can also be prepared by Williamson synthesis.
Example: C6H5ONa + CH3Br → C6H5OCH3 + NaBr. Sodium phenoxide gives anisole or methoxybenzene.
Williamson synthesis works best with primary alkyl halides.
Tertiary alkyl halides are not suitable because they mainly give alkenes due to elimination.
To prepare an unsymmetrical ether, a primary alkyl halide and a sodium alkoxide should be chosen.
For tert-butyl ethyl ether, the correct combination is tert-butoxide with ethyl bromide, because ethyl bromide is primary.

notes.heading: Physical Properties of Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
notes.text: Important physical properties include physical state, boiling point, solubility and hydrogen bonding.
points: Lower alcohols such as methanol and ethanol are colourless liquids.
Higher alcohols may be solids.
Phenol is generally a colourless crystalline solid, but it slowly turns pink or brown due to oxidation in air.
Ethers are generally colourless, volatile liquids. Example: diethyl ether.
Alcohols and phenols have higher boiling points than ethers, alkanes and haloalkanes of similar molecular mass.
Alcohols and phenols have high boiling points because the –OH group forms intermolecular hydrogen bonding.
Ethanol has a higher boiling point than dimethyl ether, although both have molecular formula C2H6O, because ethanol forms hydrogen bonds while dimethyl ether cannot form self hydrogen bonds.
For compounds of similar molecular mass, the boiling point order is: Alcohols > Ethers > Hydrocarbons.
Lower alcohols such as methanol, ethanol and propanol are soluble in water because they form hydrogen bonds with water.
As the size of the alkyl group increases, solubility in water decreases because the alkyl group is hydrophobic.
Solubility order: Methanol > Ethanol > Propanol > Butanol.
Phenol is partly soluble in water because it can form hydrogen bonds with water, but the benzene ring is hydrophobic.
Ethers are slightly soluble in water because the oxygen atom has lone pairs and can accept hydrogen bonds from water.
Ethers cannot form hydrogen bonds among themselves because they do not have an O–H bond.
More branching decreases boiling point because branching decreases surface area and weakens van der Waals forces.
More branching increases solubility in water because the hydrocarbon part becomes more compact.
Among butyl alcohols, tert-butyl alcohol is more soluble in water than n-butyl alcohol.

notes.heading: Chemical Reactions of Alcohols
notes.text: Alcohols react mainly due to the O–H bond and the C–O bond.
points: Alcohols have the general formula R–OH.
Alcohols react with sodium metal to form sodium alkoxide and hydrogen gas.
General reaction: 2R–OH + 2Na → 2R–ONa + H2.
Example: 2CH3CH2OH + 2Na → 2CH3CH2ONa + H2. Ethanol forms sodium ethoxide.
The reaction with sodium shows that alcohols are weakly acidic.
Alcohols react with carboxylic acids in the presence of concentrated H2SO4 to form esters. This is esterification.
General reaction: R–OH + R′COOH → R′COOR + H2O.
Example: CH3CH2OH + CH3COOH → CH3COOC2H5 + H2O. Ethanol and ethanoic acid form ethyl ethanoate.
Esters usually have a pleasant fruity smell.
Alcohols react with hydrogen halides such as HCl, HBr and HI to form haloalkanes.
General reaction: R–OH + HX → R–X + H2O.
Example: CH3CH2OH + HBr → CH3CH2Br + H2O. Ethanol gives bromoethane.
Reactivity order of hydrogen halides: HI > HBr > HCl.
Reactivity order of alcohols towards hydrogen halides: Tertiary alcohol > Secondary alcohol > Primary alcohol.
Alcohols react with PCl5 to form alkyl chlorides. General reaction: R–OH + PCl5 → R–Cl + POCl3 + HCl.
Example: C2H5OH + PCl5 → C2H5Cl + POCl3 + HCl.
Alcohols react with PCl3 to form alkyl chlorides. General reaction: 3R–OH + PCl3 → 3R–Cl + H3PO3.
Alcohols react with thionyl chloride to form alkyl chlorides. General reaction: R–OH + SOCl2 → R–Cl + SO2 + HCl.
SOCl2 is preferred because the by-products SO2 and HCl are gases and escape, giving pure alkyl chloride.
Alcohols undergo dehydration when heated with concentrated H2SO4 at 443 K to form alkenes.
Example: CH3CH2OH → CH2=CH2 + H2O. Ethanol gives ethene.
Dehydration order: Tertiary alcohol > Secondary alcohol > Primary alcohol.
Alcohols can be oxidised using oxidising agents such as KMnO4 or K2Cr2O7/H+.
Primary alcohols first give aldehydes, then carboxylic acids. General reaction: RCH2OH → RCHO → RCOOH.
Example: CH3CH2OH → CH3CHO → CH3COOH. Ethanol gives ethanal, then ethanoic acid.
Secondary alcohols give ketones on oxidation. General reaction: R2CHOH → R2C=O.
Example: CH3CH(OH)CH3 → CH3COCH3. Propan-2-ol gives propanone.
Tertiary alcohols are difficult to oxidise; under strong conditions they may undergo breaking of carbon-carbon bonds.

notes.heading: Chemical Reactions of Phenols
notes.text: Phenols show reactions due to the –OH group and the activated benzene ring.
points: Phenols have the general formula Ar–OH. Example: C6H5OH.
Phenol is weakly acidic and can lose H+ to form phenoxide ion.
Reaction: C6H5OH ⇌ C6H5O− + H+.
Phenoxide ion is resonance stabilised, so phenol is more acidic than alcohols.
Phenol reacts with sodium metal to form sodium phenoxide and hydrogen gas.
Reaction: 2C6H5OH + 2Na → 2C6H5ONa + H2.
Phenol reacts with sodium hydroxide to form sodium phenoxide.
Reaction: C6H5OH + NaOH → C6H5ONa + H2O.
Acidity order: Carboxylic acid > Phenol > Alcohol.
Phenol reacts with acid chlorides or acid anhydrides to form esters.
Reaction with acetyl chloride: C6H5OH + CH3COCl → CH3COOC6H5 + HCl. Product is phenyl ethanoate.
Reaction with acetic anhydride: C6H5OH + (CH3CO)2O → CH3COOC6H5 + CH3COOH. This is acetylation of phenol.
The –OH group activates the benzene ring and increases electron density at ortho and para positions.
Phenol mainly gives ortho and para substituted products in electrophilic substitution reactions.
With dilute HNO3 at low temperature, phenol gives o-nitrophenol and p-nitrophenol.
o-Nitrophenol is steam volatile due to intramolecular hydrogen bonding.
p-Nitrophenol is less volatile due to intermolecular hydrogen bonding.
With concentrated HNO3, phenol gives 2,4,6-trinitrophenol, also called picric acid.
Phenol reacts with bromine water to form 2,4,6-tribromophenol as a white precipitate.
Reaction: C6H5OH + 3Br2 → C6H2Br3OH + 3HBr.
Bromine water test is an important test for phenol.
In Kolbe’s reaction, sodium phenoxide reacts with CO2 under pressure, followed by acidification to form salicylic acid.
Kolbe product: salicylic acid or o-hydroxybenzoic acid.
Salicylic acid is used in the preparation of aspirin.
In Reimer-Tiemann reaction, phenol reacts with chloroform and sodium hydroxide followed by acidification to form salicylaldehyde.
Reimer-Tiemann product: salicylaldehyde or o-hydroxybenzaldehyde.
In Reimer-Tiemann reaction, the –CHO group is introduced mainly at the ortho position.
Phenol can be oxidised to quinone using oxidising agents such as chromic acid.

notes.heading: Chemical Reactions of Ethers
notes.text: Ethers are generally less reactive than alcohols and phenols, but they undergo cleavage by strong hydrogen halides and electrophilic substitution in aryl ethers.
points: Ethers have the general formula R–O–R′.
Ethers react with strong hydrogen halides such as HI and HBr, causing cleavage of the C–O bond.
General reaction: R–O–R′ + HX → R–X + R′–OH, where HX may be HI or HBr.
Example: CH3–O–CH3 + HI → CH3I + CH3OH.
With excess HI, the alcohol formed further reacts to form another alkyl iodide.
Example: CH3–O–CH3 + 2HI → 2CH3I + H2O.
In unsymmetrical ethers, cleavage usually occurs so that halide ion attacks the smaller or less hindered alkyl group.
Example: CH3–O–C2H5 + HI → CH3I + C2H5OH.
If one alkyl group is tertiary, the halide is formed from the tertiary group.
Example: (CH3)3C–O–CH3 + HI → (CH3)3C–I + CH3OH.
Tertiary cleavage occurs because a tertiary carbocation is stable.
Anisole is C6H5–O–CH3.
Anisole reacts with HI to form phenol and methyl iodide.
Reaction: C6H5OCH3 + HI → C6H5OH + CH3I.
In anisole, the O–C6H5 bond does not break easily because it has partial double bond character due to resonance. Cleavage occurs at the alkyl–oxygen bond.
Anisole contains the –OCH3 group, which is activating and ortho-para directing.
Bromination of anisole gives o-bromoanisole and p-bromoanisole, with p-bromoanisole as the major product.
Nitration of anisole with HNO3/H2SO4 gives o-nitroanisole and p-nitroanisole, with p-nitroanisole as the major product.
Anisole undergoes Friedel-Crafts alkylation and acylation.
Example: Anisole + CH3Cl/AlCl3 gives o-methylanisole and p-methylanisole, with the para product usually major.

notes.heading: Acidity of Alcohols and Phenols
notes.text: Alcohols and phenols both contain the –OH group, but phenols are more acidic than alcohols.
points: Alcohols are very weakly acidic and can lose H+ to form alkoxide ions.
General reaction: R–OH ⇌ R–O− + H+.
Example: CH3CH2OH ⇌ CH3CH2O− + H+.
Alcohols react with sodium metal to form sodium alkoxide and hydrogen gas.
Reaction: 2R–OH + 2Na → 2R–ONa + H2.
Example: 2C2H5OH + 2Na → 2C2H5ONa + H2.
Alcohols are weaker acids than water and phenols.
Phenols are more acidic than alcohols because phenoxide ion is resonance stabilised.
Reaction: C6H5OH ⇌ C6H5O− + H+.
Phenol reacts with sodium metal to give sodium phenoxide and hydrogen gas.
Reaction: 2C6H5OH + 2Na → 2C6H5ONa + H2.
Phenol reacts with NaOH to form sodium phenoxide.
Reaction: C6H5OH + NaOH → C6H5ONa + H2O.
In phenoxide ion, the negative charge is delocalised over the benzene ring by resonance, making the ion stable.
In alkoxide ions, the negative charge remains mainly on oxygen and there is no resonance stabilisation.
Alkyl groups show +I effect, increasing electron density on oxygen and making alkoxide ions less stable.
General acidity order: Phenol > Water > Alcohol.
Alcohol acidity order: Methanol > Primary alcohol > Secondary alcohol > Tertiary alcohol.
Acidity of alcohols decreases as alkyl groups increase because the +I effect increases.
Electron-withdrawing groups such as –NO2, –CN, –CHO and –COOH increase the acidity of phenols by stabilising phenoxide ion.
Nitrophenol is more acidic than phenol.
Electron-donating groups such as –CH3 and –OCH3 decrease the acidity of phenols by destabilising phenoxide ion.
Cresol is less acidic than phenol.
Acidity order among examples: p-nitrophenol > o-nitrophenol > phenol.
Carboxylic acids are stronger acids than phenols because carboxylate ions are more resonance stabilised than phenoxide ions.
Overall acidity order: Carboxylic acid > Phenol > Alcohol.

notes.heading: Tests to Distinguish Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
notes.text: Alcohols, phenols and ethers can be distinguished using simple chemical tests.
points: Sodium metal test is given by alcohols and phenols because both contain acidic hydrogen.
General reaction for alcohols: 2R–OH + 2Na → 2R–ONa + H2.
Reaction for phenol: 2C6H5OH + 2Na → 2C6H5ONa + H2.
Observation in sodium metal test: bubbles of hydrogen gas are produced.
Ethers do not give sodium metal test because they do not have acidic hydrogen.
Phenols give a violet colour with neutral FeCl3 solution.
Neutral FeCl3 test is used to identify phenols.
Alcohols and ethers generally do not give violet colour with neutral FeCl3.
Phenol reacts with bromine water to form 2,4,6-tribromophenol.
Reaction: C6H5OH + 3Br2 → C6H2Br3OH + 3HBr.
Observation in bromine water test: bromine water is decolourised and a white precipitate is formed.
Lucas test distinguishes primary, secondary and tertiary alcohols.
Lucas reagent is concentrated HCl and anhydrous ZnCl2.
Alcohols react with Lucas reagent to form insoluble alkyl chlorides, causing turbidity.
Tertiary alcohol gives immediate turbidity in Lucas test.
Secondary alcohol gives turbidity after a few minutes in Lucas test.
Primary alcohol gives no turbidity at room temperature in Lucas test.
Reactivity order in Lucas test: 3° alcohol > 2° alcohol > 1° alcohol.
Victor Meyer test distinguishes alcohols by colour.
Primary alcohol gives red colour in Victor Meyer test.
Secondary alcohol gives blue colour in Victor Meyer test.
Tertiary alcohol gives no colour in Victor Meyer test.
Iodoform test is given by alcohols containing the group CH3–CH(OH)–.
Reagents for iodoform test are I2 and NaOH.
Observation in iodoform test: yellow precipitate of iodoform, CHI3, is formed.
Ethanol gives iodoform test because it can be oxidised to ethanal.
Propan-2-ol gives iodoform test because it contains the CH3–CH(OH)– group.
Ethanol is the only primary alcohol that gives iodoform test.
Ethers do not react with sodium metal, do not give FeCl3 test, and do not give bromine water test like phenol.
Ethers can be identified by cleavage with hot concentrated HI.
Reaction: R–O–R′ + HI → R–I + R′–OH.
With excess HI: R–O–R′ + 2HI → R–I + R′–I + H2O.

notes.heading: Important Named Reactions
notes.text: Named reactions from this chapter are important for exams and conversions.
points: Kolbe’s reaction is shown by phenol.
In Kolbe’s reaction, phenol is first converted into sodium phenoxide by NaOH.
Reaction: C6H5OH + NaOH → C6H5ONa + H2O.
Sodium phenoxide reacts with CO2 under pressure and heat to form sodium salicylate.
After acidification, sodium salicylate gives salicylic acid.
Kolbe’s reaction introduces –COOH mainly at the ortho position of phenol.
Product of Kolbe’s reaction is salicylic acid or 2-hydroxybenzoic acid.
Reimer-Tiemann reaction is shown by phenol.
In Reimer-Tiemann reaction, phenol reacts with chloroform and sodium hydroxide followed by acidification to give salicylaldehyde.
Reimer-Tiemann reaction introduces –CHO mainly at the ortho position of phenol.
Product of Reimer-Tiemann reaction is salicylaldehyde or 2-hydroxybenzaldehyde.
Williamson ether synthesis is used to prepare ethers.
General reaction: R–ONa + R′–X → R–O–R′ + NaX.
Example: C2H5ONa + CH3Br → CH3OC2H5 + NaBr.
Williamson synthesis works best with primary alkyl halides; tertiary alkyl halides mainly give alkenes due to elimination.
Esterification is the reaction of alcohols with carboxylic acids in the presence of concentrated H2SO4 to form esters.
General reaction: R–OH + R′COOH → R′COOR + H2O.
Example: C2H5OH + CH3COOH → CH3COOC2H5 + H2O.
Acetylation of phenol is the reaction of phenol with acetic anhydride to form phenyl ethanoate.
Reaction: C6H5OH + (CH3CO)2O → CH3COOC6H5 + CH3COOH.
Dehydration of alcohols occurs when alcohols lose water in the presence of concentrated H2SO4.
At 443 K, ethanol gives ethene: C2H5OH → CH2=CH2 + H2O.
At 413 K, ethanol gives ether: 2C2H5OH → C2H5OC2H5 + H2O.

notes.heading: Important Conversions
notes.text: Conversions involve changing one organic compound into another using suitable reagents.
points: Alcohol to alkene: Alcohols give alkenes by dehydration using concentrated H2SO4 at 443 K. Example: CH3CH2OH → CH2=CH2 + H2O.
Alcohol to haloalkane: Alcohols react with HCl/ZnCl2, HBr, PCl5 or SOCl2 to form haloalkanes. Example: CH3CH2OH + HBr → CH3CH2Br + H2O.
Alcohol to aldehyde: Primary alcohols on controlled oxidation give aldehydes. Reagent: PCC. Example: CH3CH2OH → CH3CHO.
Alcohol to carboxylic acid: Primary alcohols on strong oxidation give carboxylic acids. Reagents: acidified KMnO4 or acidified K2Cr2O7. Example: CH3CH2OH → CH3COOH.
Secondary alcohol to ketone: Secondary alcohols on oxidation give ketones. Reagents: acidified K2Cr2O7 or KMnO4. Example: CH3CH(OH)CH3 → CH3COCH3.
Alkene to alcohol by Markovnikov hydration: Reagent H2O/H+. Example: CH3CH=CH2 + H2O → CH3CH(OH)CH3.
Alkene to alcohol by anti-Markovnikov hydroboration-oxidation: Reagents B2H6 and H2O2/OH−. Example: CH3CH=CH2 → CH3CH2CH2OH.
Phenol to sodium phenoxide: C6H5OH + NaOH → C6H5ONa + H2O.
Sodium phenoxide to phenol: C6H5ONa + HCl → C6H5OH + NaCl.
Phenol to salicylic acid: Use Kolbe’s reaction with NaOH, CO2 under pressure, followed by acidification.
Phenol to salicylaldehyde: Use Reimer-Tiemann reaction with CHCl3 and NaOH, followed by acidification.
Phenol to picric acid: Treat phenol with concentrated HNO3 to form 2,4,6-trinitrophenol.
Phenol to 2,4,6-tribromophenol: Treat phenol with bromine water. Reaction: C6H5OH + 3Br2 → C6H2Br3OH + 3HBr.
Alcohol to ether: Intermolecular dehydration using concentrated H2SO4 at 413 K. Example: 2C2H5OH → C2H5–O–C2H5 + H2O.
Alkoxide to ether: Use Williamson synthesis. General reaction: R–ONa + R′–X → R–O–R′ + NaX.
Sodium phenoxide to anisole: C6H5ONa + CH3Br → C6H5OCH3 + NaBr.
Anisole to phenol: C6H5OCH3 + HI → C6H5OH + CH3I.
Important temperature rule: 413 K gives ether and 443 K gives alkene.

notes.heading: Important Exam Points
notes.text: These points are frequently useful for board exam revision.
points: Alcohols and phenols are soluble in water because the –OH group forms hydrogen bonds with water molecules.
As the carbon chain increases, solubility in water decreases due to the hydrophobic alkyl group.
Solubility order: Methanol > Ethanol > Propanol > Butanol.
Alcohols have higher boiling points than ethers of similar molecular mass because alcohols form intermolecular hydrogen bonding, while ethers cannot form hydrogen bonds among themselves.
Phenol is more acidic than alcohol because phenoxide ion is resonance stabilised, while alkoxide ion is not resonance stabilised.
Phenol is less acidic than carboxylic acid because carboxylate ion is more resonance stabilised than phenoxide ion.
Phenol reacts with NaOH but alcohol generally does not, because phenol is more acidic than alcohol.
The –OH group in phenol is activating and directs electrophilic substitution to ortho and para positions.
Phenol gives a white precipitate with bromine water due to formation of 2,4,6-tribromophenol.
Kolbe’s reaction converts phenol into salicylic acid using NaOH, CO2 under pressure, and acidification.
Reimer-Tiemann reaction converts phenol into salicylaldehyde using CHCl3, NaOH and acidification.
Williamson ether synthesis prepares ethers from sodium alkoxide and alkyl halide.
Williamson synthesis works best with primary alkyl halides.
Tertiary alkyl halides mainly give alkenes due to elimination in Williamson synthesis.
Anisole reacts with HI to give phenol and methyl iodide because the O–CH3 bond breaks, while the O–C6H5 bond is difficult to break due to partial double bond character.
At 413 K with concentrated H2SO4, alcohol gives ether.
At 443 K with concentrated H2SO4, alcohol gives alkene.
Primary alcohols oxidise to aldehydes and then carboxylic acids.
Secondary alcohols oxidise to ketones.
Tertiary alcohols are difficult to oxidise.
Lucas reagent is concentrated HCl and anhydrous ZnCl2.
In Lucas test, tertiary alcohol gives immediate turbidity, secondary alcohol gives turbidity after some time, and primary alcohol gives no turbidity at room temperature.
Iodoform test gives yellow precipitate of CHI3 with ethanol and alcohols containing the CH3–CH(OH)– group.
Phenol with FeCl3 gives violet colour.
Phenol with concentrated HNO3 gives picric acid or 2,4,6-trinitrophenol.
SOCl2 is preferred for converting alcohols to alkyl chlorides because SO2 and HCl are gaseous by-products and escape.

notes.heading: Complete Chapter Quick Revision
notes.text: Alcohols contain –OH attached to alkyl carbon, phenols contain –OH attached to benzene ring, and ethers contain –O– between two carbon groups.
points: Functional groups: Alcohol is R–OH, phenol is Ar–OH, and ether is R–O–R′.
Examples: alcohol C2H5OH, phenol C6H5OH, ether CH3OCH3.
Alcohol classification: primary alcohol R–CH2OH, secondary alcohol R–CH(OH)–R′, tertiary alcohol R3C–OH.
Phenol classification: monohydric phenol such as phenol, dihydric phenols such as catechol, resorcinol and hydroquinone, and trihydric phenol such as pyrogallol.
Ether classification: simple ethers have the same groups on both sides of oxygen, and mixed ethers have different groups on both sides of oxygen.
Alcohol preparation from alkenes: acid hydration follows Markovnikov’s rule; hydroboration-oxidation follows anti-Markovnikov addition.
Alcohol preparation from carbonyl compounds: aldehydes give primary alcohols and ketones give secondary alcohols on reduction.
Alcohol preparation by Grignard reagent: formaldehyde gives primary alcohol, aldehyde gives secondary alcohol, and ketone gives tertiary alcohol.

Phenol preparation: from chlorobenzene using NaOH at high temperature and pressure followed by acidification, from benzene diazonium chloride using warm water, from cumene by oxidation and acid hydrolysis, and from benzene sulphonic acid by NaOH fusion and acidification.
Ether preparation: alcohols at 413 K with concentrated H2SO4 give ethers; sodium alkoxides and alkyl halides give ethers by Williamson synthesis.
Alcohols and phenols have high boiling points due to intermolecular hydrogen bonding.
Ethers have lower boiling points than alcohols because ethers cannot form hydrogen bonds among themselves.
Lower alcohols are soluble in water due to hydrogen bonding, and solubility decreases as carbon chain length increases.
Phenol is partly soluble in water and ethers are slightly soluble in water.
Alcohol reactions include reaction with sodium, esterification, reaction with HX, reaction with PCl5, reaction with SOCl2, dehydration and oxidation.
Phenol reactions include reaction with sodium, reaction with NaOH, nitration, bromination, Kolbe reaction, Reimer-Tiemann reaction and oxidation.
Ether reactions include cleavage by HI or HBr and electrophilic substitution in anisole.
Anisole with HI gives phenol and methyl iodide.
General acidity order: Carboxylic acid > Phenol > Water > Alcohol.
Phenol is more acidic than alcohol due to resonance stabilisation of phenoxide ion.
Alcohol acidity order: Methanol > Primary alcohol > Secondary alcohol > Tertiary alcohol.
Electron-withdrawing groups increase acidity of phenols, while electron-donating groups decrease acidity.
Important tests: sodium metal test for alcohols and phenols, FeCl3 test for phenol, bromine water test for phenol, Lucas test for alcohols, and iodoform test for ethanol and CH3–CH(OH)– group.

Most important named reactions: Kolbe reaction gives salicylic acid, Reimer-Tiemann reaction gives salicylaldehyde, Williamson synthesis gives ether, esterification gives ester, and acetylation of phenol gives phenyl ethanoate.

Important memory line: Alcohol at 413 K gives ether, alcohol at 443 K gives alkene, Kolbe reaction gives salicylic acid, Reimer-Tiemann reaction gives salicylaldehyde, and Williamson synthesis gives ether.
